original_hypothesis.title: GlobalRouter.exactInput Is a No-Op That Silently Returns Zero and Traps ETH
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GlobalRouter.exactInput has its entire function body commented out, returning amountOut=0 silently. The function is marked payable, so any ETH sent with the call is permanently locked in the GlobalRouter contract which has no withdrawal mechanism. ERC20 calls would silently return 0 output without moving any tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function body contains only commented-out code. The payable modifier allows ETH to be received. GlobalRouter has no receive/fallback (except via payable functions) and no ETH withdrawal function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function body is entirely commented out between /* and */; returns default uint256(0) for amountOut; payable modifier accepts ETH

preconditions: For ETH loss: user sends msg.value > 0 with the call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Entire function is dead code wrapped in comments; payable allows ETH receipt with no processing
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — no fix path without redeployment

operational_questions: Is GlobalRouter.exactInput referenced by any frontend or integration?
Is there any ETH balance stuck in the GlobalRouter?
validation_requirements: Call exactInput with ETH value — verify ETH is permanently stuck
Verify no withdrawal function exists on GlobalRouter

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User calls exactInput expecting V3-style multi-hop swap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Any ETH sent is permanently locked in GlobalRouter

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: V3-style swaps via GlobalRouter completely non-functional; ETH permanently lost

validation_result.mechanism: dead code no-op with ETH trap
validation_result.impact_summary: GlobalRouter.exactInput has its entire body commented out, silently returning 0 for amountOut. Since it is payable, any ETH sent is permanently locked in the contract with no withdrawal mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The GlobalRouter.exactInput function at lines 85-112 of GlobalRouter.sol has its entire logic wrapped in a block comment (/* ... */). The only executable statement is `address payer = msg.sender;` which is unused. The return variable `amountOut` is never assigned, so it defaults to uint256(0). The function is marked `payable`, meaning it accepts ETH. There is no `receive()` function, no `fallback()` function, and no ETH withdrawal mechanism anywhere in the GlobalRouter contract. The internal `_safeTransferETH` helper exists but is never exposed through any external function that could recover trapped ETH. Any caller sending ETH with this function permanently loses it. Any caller expecting a swap to occur receives 0 output tokens silently.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at GlobalRouter.sol lines 85-112 is accurate. The function body is entirely within a block comment. The `payable` modifier is present on line 88. No withdrawal mechanism exists anywhere in the contract. The constructor only sets `tradeHelper` and no admin/owner pattern exists to add recovery functions later.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The entire function body is commented out. The function is payable, accepts ETH, does nothing, and returns 0. The amountOutMinimum check is also commented out, so no revert occurs even when 0 is returned. ETH sent with the call is permanently trapped.

calling_sequence: 2. ETH (X wei) is received by the contract due to `payable`
4. Entire swap logic is commented out between /* and */
6. No revert occurs because amountOutMinimum check is also commented out
7. ETH remains in GlobalRouter contract with no recovery path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry is GlobalRouter.exactInput at line 85. The function is declared `external payable` at line 88, accepting ETH. At line 92, `address payer = msg.sender;` is the only executable line. Lines 94-111 are wrapped in /* */ block comment. The return variable `amountOut` at line 89 is never assigned, defaulting to 0. The contract has no receive() function, no fallback() function, no withdraw function, and no owner/admin role. The internal _safeTransferETH helper at line 243 is never called from any external function that could rescue ETH.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for GlobalRouter were found. The constructor takes only a tradeHelper address.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1 (ETH loss): User calls exactInput with msg.value > 0 expecting a V3-style multi-hop swap. Function does nothing, returns 0, ETH is permanently locked. Scenario 2 (silent failure): User calls exactInput with ERC20 tokens pre-approved. Function does nothing, returns 0, no tokens are moved. User's tokens remain but the caller may have already sent tokens to the contract or approved spending. The silent return of 0 without revert is the critical issue - callers receive no error indication.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The function is publicly accessible and payable. Any ETH sent is permanently locked with no recovery mechanism. The silent return of 0 (with the amountOutMinimum check also commented out) means integrators or users calling this function will not receive an error. If any frontend or integration contract relies on GlobalRouter.exactInput, it is completely non-functional. The contract has no owner, no admin, and no upgrade mechanism, making recovery impossible without a full redeployment.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the `payable` modifier from exactInput since it does not handle ETH
Either implement the function body or remove it entirely with a revert('NOT_IMPLEMENTED')
Add an ETH recovery function with access control, or add a receive/fallback that reverts
Consider deploying a new GlobalRouter with the fix and deprecating this one
If the function is intentionally disabled, it should revert explicitly rather than silently returning 0
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GlobalRouter with any valid tradeHelper address. 2. Call exactInput{value: 1 ether}(params) with any params. 3. Verify the function returns amountOut=0. 4. Verify address(globalRouter).balance == 1 ether. 5. Verify there is no function on GlobalRouter that can extract this ETH.
uncertainties: Whether GlobalRouter.exactInput is actually used by any frontend or integration in production
Whether there is ETH already trapped in a deployed GlobalRouter instance
additional_testing_needed: Check on-chain if GlobalRouter is deployed and if it holds any ETH balance
Verify whether any frontend or SDK routes calls through GlobalRouter.exactInput
Test that ERC20 token scenarios also silently fail without moving tokens
